---
format: 
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    # code-link: true
    highlight-style: a11y
    chalkboard: true
    theme: 
      - ../../meds-slides-styles.scss
---

## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"} 

[EDS 212: Day 1, Lecture 1]{.custom-title}

[*Course intro, algebra refresher*]{.custom-subtitle}

<hr class="hr-teal">

[August 3^th^, 2026]{.custom-subtitle3}

---

## {#eds212-basics data-menu-title="EDS 212 basics" background-color="#003660"}


<div class="page-center">
<div class="custom-subtitle">EDS 212 basics</div>
</div>

---

## {#welcome data-menu-title="Welcome to EDS 212!"} 

[Welcome to EDS 212 - Essential Math in Environmental Data Science]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

- **Instructor:** Carmen Galaz García 
- **Teaching assistant:** Cella Schnabel
- **Course hours:** 10am - 4:30pm PST
- **Location:** NCEAS 1st Floor Classroom

---

## {#about-instructor data-menu-title="About your instructor"}

[About your instructor]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}


[Carmen Galaz García (she/her)]{.body-text-m .blue-text}

- Assistant Teaching Professor @ Bren

Before:

- Data Scienist @ NCEAS
- Ph.D. in Mathematics @ UCSB

**Teaching & Research**

- Python courses and Capstone Projects for Masters in Environmental Data Science (MEDS)
- Supporting math and data science across Bren!
- NCEAS/TNC collaboration for invasive iceplant mapping analyzing remotely sensed images

:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
![](/images/carmen.png){fig-align="center" width="70%"}

![](/images/bren-hex.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::

---

## {#about-TA data-menu-title="About your TA"}

[About your TA]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}


[Cella Schnabel]{.body-text-m .blue-text}


:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
![](/images/cella.png){fig-align="center" width="70%"}

![](/images/bren-hex.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::

---

## {#about-you data-menu-title="About you"} 

[About you!]{.slide-title}

<hr>


![image: Flaticon.com](images/diversity.png)



--- 

## {#course-description data-menu-title="Course description"} 

[Course description]{.slide-title}

<hr>

- **Units:** 2
- **Grading:** Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory
- **Description:** Quantitative skills are critical when working with, understanding, analyzing and gleaning insights from environmental data. In the intensive EDS 212 course, students will refresh fundamental skills in math (algebra, uni- and multivariate functions, units and unit conversions), summary statistics and basic probability theory, derivative and differential equations, linear algebra, and reading, writing and evaluating logical operations.

. . .

- This is an **"offline course"**, your only one in MEDS. What it means is we'll be focusing on conceptual understanding and some pen-and-paper practice, instead of coding. Why? We want to make sure you *understand* some fundamental topics before jumping into *implementing*. 

. . .

::: {.center-text .body-text-l}
Let's take a look at the [course syllabus](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1yGzd_H954F_gPufJNAYPIIjEw3vDS4P0E20CRrpSrfs/edit?tab=t.0) together. 
:::

---

## {#topics data-menu-title="Topics"} 

[Topics overview]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- **Day 1:** Course introduction & math basics refresher
- **Day 2:** Exponential functions; Limits & derivatives
- **Day 3:** Integration & ODEs; Vectors and matrices
- **Day 4:** Distributions & summary statistics; Basic probability theory,
- **Day 5:** Basic stats; Boolean algebra
:::

---

## {#obj data-menu-title="Workshop Objectives"}

[Objectives (in general terms)]{.slide-title}

<hr>

![](images/limits_horst.jpg){fig-align="center"}

1.  Shake off the math dust

2.  Exercise core math skills needed to succeed in your Bren courses

3.  Explore how valuable math is to environmental data science

4.  Build a collaborative environment and get to know your cohort

---

## {#exp data-menu-title="Student Expectations"}

[Student expectations]{.slide-title}

<hr>

We expect all students will:

- Support and encourage all classmates

- Be open to learning from each other

- Bring a collaborative attitude and communicate respectfully

- See opportunities to share kownledge as a chance to deepen understanding

- Complete all in-class exercises

. . .

**Logistics**

- You are required to attend all sessions, unless there's a significant reason not to. Always communicate with your instructors.
- If you are feeling sick: stay home and take care of yourself! Please mask up if you are not feeling 100%.

---

## {#survey data-menu-title="Please fill out this survery"}

[Please fill out this survey]{.slide-title}

<hr>

![](/images/eds212-2026-entrance-survey.png){fig-align="center"}

::: {.center-text .body-text-l}
[Or you can click on this link.](https://forms.gle/Q7QUpnBALn9wPNpr6)
:::

---

## {#math-envs data-menu-title="Math in Environmental Data Science" background-color="#003660"}


<div class="page-center">
<div class="custom-subtitle">Math in Environmental Data Science</div>
</div>

---

## {#why-math data-menu-title="Why math?"} 

[Why am I taking a math class?]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>
<br>
<br>

::: {.center-text .body-text-xl}
or, 

<br>

*Why do I have to know math when a computer will do it for me?*
:::

---

## {#warm-up data-menu-title="Brain warm-up"} 

[A quantitative brain warm-up]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

::: {.center-text .body-text-l .teal-text}
MEDS students have diverse work & academic histories
:::

**This course (re)introduces math concepts and tools that:** 

- Focus on applications to environmental data science
- Are specifically relevant for MEDS projects, coursework
- Provide an entryway into building computational skills
- Refresh quantitative thinking skills generally
- Refresh essentials like units, conversions, notation, **language**

--- 

## {#des data-menu-title="describe"}

[Share with the person next to you:]{.slide-title}

<hr>

[What has been your life or professional experience with math? Best friend ever, mortal enemy, or something in between?]{.slide-title}


<br>

[How do you see math being relevant to solving environmental problems?]{.slide-title}

---

## {#math-eds data-menu-title="Math in EDS"}

[Math is a core tool in environmental data science]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<p style="text-align: center">
**Math helps us investigate the world and communicate our findings**
</p>


- Used by scientists to find evidence

- Can be used to quantify relations, trends, and changes

- Mathematical models can help us understand complex systems

- Must be used responsibly and with awareness of potential biases and limitations

- Helps us support arguments that can transform policy and actions

- At the center of tools like statistics, machine lerning, and image processing

<br>

[We need to understand the math to make decisions and efficient implementations using it!]{.center-text .body-text-m .blue-text}

---

## {#math-bren data-menu-title="Math at Bren"}

[Math at Bren]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

:::{.center-text}
**In your classes:**
:::

- EDS 222: Statistics for Environmental Data Science

- EDS 230: Modeling Environmental Systems

- EDS 232: Machine Learning in Environmental Science
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}

:::{.center-text}
**In Research**
:::

Ecology, modeling, economics, and more!

- [Modeling the Impact of Decarbonization on Labor in California’s Central Coast](https://bren.ucsb.edu/projects/modeling-impact-decarbonization-labor-californias-central-coast)

- [Quantifying Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions Associated with Global Seafood Production](https://bren.ucsb.edu/projects/quantifying-greenhouse-gas-ghg-emissions-associated-global-seafood-production)

- [Characterizing The Relationship Between Evapotranspiration and Groundwater Decline Across Arid Agricultural Regions](https://bren.ucsb.edu/projects/characterizing-relationship-between-evapotranspiration-and-groundwater-decline-across-arid)
:::
:::

---

## {#warm-up-overview data-menu-title="Warm-up overview"} 

[Today: Math brain warm up]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Algebra review
- Exponentials and logarithms
- Units
- Functions
- Understanding graphs
- Linear functions
:::

---

## {#algebra-review data-menu-title="Algebra review" background-color="#003660"}


<div class="page-center">
<div class="custom-subtitle">Algebra review</div>
</div>

---

## {#rules-of-algebra data-menu-title="Rules of algebra"} 

[Rules of algebra]{.slide-title}

<hr>

[**You can get far with a few rules:**]{.body-text-l}

::: {.body-text-m}
1.  Follow order of operations (a.k.a. PEDMAS)


2. Never change an equation. Instead, we rewrite them into more useful forms

3. Manipulate BOTH sides of an equation with the SAME PROCEDURE.

<span style="color: red;">
Always add/substract/multiply/divide by the same number on both sides. More generally, always apply the same function to both sides of the equation. 
</span>

:::

---

## {#PEMDAS data-menu-title="PEMDAS"} 

[1. Order of operations (P-E-DM-AS)]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

[**P** - Parentheses]{.body-text-l}

<br>

[**E** - Exponents]{.body-text-l}

<br>

[**D/M** - Division / multiplication]{.body-text-l}

<br>

[**A/S** - Addition / subtraction]{.body-text-l}

---

## {#PEMDAS-practice data-menu-title="PEMDAS practice"} 

[1. Order of operations practice problems]{.slide-title}

<hr>



[Simplify the following:]{.body-text-m}


<br>

* [$\frac{4-6}{2}(3+1)-\frac{1+2*4}{3}$]{.body-text-m}

<br>

* [$3x+4(8x-6x) -(2y-5)+\frac{2x(1-3)}{2}$]{.body-text-m}

<br>

* [If we are given the values of $x=-3$ and $y=2$, what would be the value of $z$?
$$
z = 4*(y-4)+(x+1)^2
$$]{.body-text-m}

✏️ [Take a minute to solve these individually.]{style="color:green;"  .body-text-m}


::: {.notes}
1. $(12 - 2)/5 + 5(3+2)/6$
- $(10/5) + 5 \times 5 / 6$
- **ANSWER: $2 + 25/6$**

2. $\frac{(4-6)}{(2)}(3+1)-\frac{(1+2*4)}{(3)}$
- $\frac{(-2)}{(2)}(4)-\frac{(9)}{(3)}$
- $-1 \times 4 - 3$
- $-4-3$
- **ANSWER: $-7$**

3. $3x+4(8x-6x) -(2y-5)+\frac{2x(1-3)}{2}$
- $3x+4 \times 2x - 2y + 5 + \frac{2x-6x}{2}$
- $3x+4 \times 2x - 2y + 5 + \frac{2x}{2}-\frac{6x}{2}$
- $3x+4 \times 2x - 2y + 5 + x - 3x$
- $11x - 2y + 5 - 2x$
- **ANSWER: $9x - 2y + 5$**

:::

---

## {#PEMDAS-practice-solution-1 data-menu-title="PEMDAS practice: solution 1"} 

[1. Order of operations practice problems]{.slide-title}

<hr>

💡 [Let's see a solution!]{style="color:green;" .body-text-m}

<br>

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="40%"}
[$\frac{4-6}{2}(3+1)-\frac{1+2*4}{3}$]{.body-text-m}
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
[$3x+4(8x-6x) -(2y-5)+\frac{2x(1-3)}{2}$]{.body-text-m}
:::

::::

---

## {#PEMDAS-practice-solution-2 data-menu-title="PEMDAS practice: solution 2"} 

[1. Order of operations practice problems]{.slide-title}

<hr>

💡 [Let's see a solution!]{style="color:green;" .body-text-m}

[If we are given the values of $x=-3$ and $y=2$, what would be the value of $z$?
$$
z = 4*(y-4)+(x+1)^2
$$]{.body-text-m}



---

## {#notation-matters data-menu-title="Notation matters"} 

[1. Notation matters]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
Simplify the following: $6 \div 3(4+2)$

What would be a harder-to-misinterpret way to write this?
:::

<br>

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "80%"
#| fig-align: "center"
knitr::include_graphics("images/pemdas-clarity.png")
```

::: {.notes}
ANSWER: 12

A harder to misinterpret way to write this: $(6 \div 3) \times (4+2)$
:::

---

## {#takeaway data-menu-title="Takeaway"} 

[1. An important takeaway:]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

::: {.center-text .body-text-l}
Being readable & hard to incorrectly interpret is often as important as being technically "correct"
:::

<br>
<br>

[When designing things, it's important to **consider the different ways that users might misuse or misunderstand it** - then **build in safeguards** to help them use it correctly. **Clear communication** and **user-centered design** is critical in environmental data science.]{.body-text-m} 


<!-- 

---

## {#equations-solved data-menu-title="Equations solved"} 

[2. Solving equations]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

[$$2x-5y+3.9=8x^2-100.7x$$]{.body-text-l}

<br>

[Provides solutions for the questions:]{.body-text-m}

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
1. "What is the value of $2x-5y+3.9$?" and 
2. "What is the value of $8x^2-100.7x$?"
::: 

-->

--- 

## {#equations-solved data-menu-title="Equations solved"} 

[2. Solving equations]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Equations help us find unknown values that satisfy certain conditions. 

Remember the rules:

2. Never change an equation. Instead, we rewrite them into more useful forms

3. Manipulate BOTH sides of an equation with the SAME PROCEDURE.

<span style="color: red;">
Always add/substract/multiply/divide by the same number on both sides. More generally, always apply the same function to both sides of the equation. 
</span>

. . . 

**Example**

Prices are important in economics, but not always available for environmental goods.


:::{.center-text}
**How do we get prices if we know quantity?**
:::

$$
\require{cancel}
\begin{aligned}
Q&=\frac{(400-P)}{80} &\text{Isolate P in terms of Q} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

✏️ [Take a minute to solve this individually.]{style="color:green;"}


---

## {#solving-equations-2 data-menu-title="Solving equations"}

[2. Solving equations]{.slide-title}

<hr>

💡 [Let's see a solution!]{style="color:green;"}

Prices are important in economics, but not always available for environmental goods. 

:::{.center-text}
**How do we get prices if we know quantity?**
:::

$$
\require{cancel}
\begin{aligned}
Q&=\frac{(400-P)}{80} &\text{Isolate P in terms of Q} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---


## {#solving-equations-3 data-menu-title="Solving equations"}

[2. Solving equations]{.slide-title}

<hr>

💡 [Let's see a solution!]{style="color:green;"}

Prices are important in economics, but not always available for environmental goods. 


:::{.center-text}
**How do we get prices if we know quantity?**
:::

$$
\require{cancel}
\begin{aligned}
Q&=\frac{(400-P)}{80} &\text{Isolate P in terms of Q} \\
80Q&=\frac{(400-P)\cancel{80}}{\cancel{80}}  &\text{ Multiply both sides by 80} \\
80Q-400&=\cancel{400}-\cancel{400} -P &\text{ Subtract both sides by 400} \\
-1(80Q-400)&=-P(-1) &\text{Multiply both sides by -1} \\
400-80Q&=P  &\text{Flip terms for simplicity}
\end{aligned}
$$

## {#practice-alge-1 data-menu-title="Easy to make mistakes"}

[It can be easy to make mistakes while doing algebra. Practice makes perfect!]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Solve all in terms of $x$

(A)  $3x+2=10x-12$

<br>

(B) $4-3(2x+1)=8-\frac{3x}{2}$

<br>

(C) $3(x+7a)-5=b+2(c-4x)$

✏️ [Take a minute to solve these individually.]{style="color:green;"}

## {#practice-alge-2 data-menu-title="Easy to make mistakes"}

[It can be easy to make mistakes while doing algebra. Practice makes perfect!]{.slide-title}

<hr>

💡 [Let's see a solution!]{style="color:green;"}

Solve all in terms of $x$

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
:::{.center-text}
(A)  $3x+2=10x-12$
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
:::{.center-text}
(B) $4-3(2x+1)=8-\frac{3x}{2}$
:::
:::

::::
## {#practice-alge-3 data-menu-title="Easy to make mistakes"}

[It can be easy to make mistakes while doing algebra. Practice makes perfect!]{.slide-title}

<hr>

💡 [Let's see a solution!]{style="color:green;"}

Solve all in terms of $x$

(C) $3(x+7a)-5=b+2(c-4x)$


## {#practice-alge-4 data-menu-title="Solutions"}

[Practice Solutions]{.slide-title}

<hr>

:::columns

:::{.column width=50%}
$$
\small
\begin{aligned}
3x+2&=10x-12 \\
3x+2+12&=10x-12(+12) \\
3x-3x+14&=10x-3x \\
14&=7x \\
x&=2
\end{aligned}
$$
<br>

$$
\small
\begin{aligned}
4-3(2x+1)&=8-\frac{3x}{2}\\
4-3-6x&=8-\frac{3x}{2} \\
1-6x&=8-\frac{3x}{2} \\
2-12x&=16-3x \\
-9x&=14 \\
x&=\frac{-14}{9}\\
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

:::{.column width=50%}
$$
\small
\begin{aligned}
3(x+7a)-5&=b+2(c-4x)\\
3x+21a-5&=b+2c-8x \\
11x+21a-5&=b+2c \\
11x&=5+b+2c-21a\\
x&=\frac{5+b+2c-21a}{11}
\end{aligned}
$$
:::
:::


---

## {#next-exp data-menu-title="Next, exponentials"} 

[Math brain warm up]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Algebra blitz
- [**Exponentials**]{.teal-text} and logarithms (this afternoon)
- Common units and unit conversions
- Functions
- Understanding graphs
- Interpreting equations
:::

---

## {#exponents data-menu-title="Exponents"} 

[Exponents & how to do math with them]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-l}
$$x^n=x\times x\times\ x \times x...(n\space times)$$

<br>

<!-- [Exponent warm-ups]{.body-text-m} -->

Evaluate the following to find a value for $y$:

<br>

1. $y = 12-2^4$

2. $2y + 30=y+3^3$
:::

::: {.notes}
Sometimes things are related to each other in an exponential way, so we're going to remind ourselves how they work using numbers.

1. $y = 12-2^4$
- $y = 12 - (2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 2)$
- $y = 12 - 26$
- **ANSWER: $y = -4$**

2. $2y + 30=y+3^3$
- $2y + 30 = y + (3 \times 3 \times 3)$
- $2y + 30 = y + 27$
- $y + 30 = 27$
- **ANSWER: $y = -3$**
:::

---

## {#exponents-rules data-menu-title="Exponent rules"} 

[Exponent rules]{.slide-title}

<hr>

- $x^ax^b=x^{a+b}$ ; **Example:** $x^5x^3=x^{5+3}=x^8$ ; **Example:** $2^2 \times 2^2$ = $(2 \times 2) \times (2 \times 2)$ = $2^4$

. . .

- $\frac{x^a}{x^b}=x^{a-b}$ ; **Example:** $\frac{z^5}{z^3}=z^{5-3}=z^2$ ; **Example:** $\frac{2^3}{2^2}$ = $\frac{2 \times 2 \times 2}{2 \times 2}$ = $2^1$

. . .

- $\frac{1}{x^a}=x^{-a}$ ; **Example:** $b^{-4x}=\frac{1}{b^{4x}}$

. . .

- $(x^a)^b=x^{ab}$ ; **Example:** $(2^3)^2=2^{3*2}=2^6=64$

. . .

- $(\frac{x}{y})^a=\frac{x^a}{y^a}$ ; **Example:** $(\frac{y}{2^2})^2=\frac{y^2}{(2^2)^2}=\frac{y^2}{2^4}=\frac{y^2}{16}$

. . .

- $(xy)^a=x^ay^a$ ; **Example:** $(3x)^2=3^2x^2$

---

## {#exponents-practice data-menu-title="Exponent practice"} 

[Exponent practice]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

[Simplify the following expressions using the rules of exponents:]{.body-text-l}

<br>

[1. $3x^5x^8x^{-11}$]{.body-text-l}

<br>

[2. $\frac{-8x^6}{2x^4}+7x^2$]{.body-text-l}

<br>

[3. $\frac{3x}{x^5}-3.8x^4\frac{x^3}{x^6}+8.1x-11.2$]{.body-text-l}

::: {.notes}
1. $3x^5x^8x^{-11}$
- **ANSWER: $3x^2$**

2. $\frac{-8x^6}{2x^4}+7x^2$
- $-4x^2 + 7x^2$
- **ANSWER: $3x^2$**

3. $\frac{3x}{x^5}-3.8x^4\frac{x^3}{x^6}+8.1x-11.2$
- $3x^{-4}-3.8x^4 \times x^{-3} + 8.1x - 11.2$
- $3x^{-4}-3.8x^1 + 8.1x - 11.2$
- **ANSWER: $3x^{-4}+4.3x - 11.2$**
:::

---

## {#FOIL data-menu-title="FOIL"} 

[Multiplying expressions (FOIL)]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

[**F**irst, **O**utside, **I**nside, **L**ast]{.body-text-l}

<br>

[**Example:**]{.body-text-l}

[$$(2x+5)(x-3)$$]{.body-text-l}

. . .

<!-- [$$=2x^2-3*2x+5*x-5*3$$]{.body-text-l} -->

[$$= (2x \times x) (2x \times -3) (5 \times x) (5 \times -3)$$]{.body-text-l}

[$$= 2x^2 - 6x + 5x - 15$$]{.body-text-l}

[$$=2x^2-x-15$$]{.body-text-l}

---

## {#next-units data-menu-title="Next, units"} 

[Math brain warm up]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Algebra blitz
- Exponentials and logarithms
- [**Common units and unit conversions**]{.teal-text}
- Functions
- Understanding graphs
- Interpreting equations
:::

---

## {#units data-menu-title="Units"} 

[UNITS. UNITS. UNITS.]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
Think about these statements, which all contain the same *value* of 4:

<br>

- There are four in the refrigerator.

- There are four **burritos** in the refrigerator.

- There are four **roaches** in the refrigerator.

- There are four **million dollars** in the refrigerator.
:::

---

## {#units-critical data-menu-title="Units are critical"} 

[Units are critical in environmental data science]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

<br>
<br>

::: {.center-text .body-text-l}
We cannot responsibly work with data without knowing the units of **each variable we're working with**.

<br>

That means we need to always **familiarize ourselves with metadata**, carefully **check units and any unit conversions**, and understand **how units combine into the units of a dependent variable**.
:::

---

## {#unit-conv data-menu-title="Unit conversions"} 

[Dimensional analysis for unit conversions]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
In dimensional analysis, we multiply initial units by a sequence of conversion factors to arrive at the final desired units.

<br>

For example, to convert $100 \frac{g}{cm^3}$ into units of $\frac{kg}{in^3}$, given that 1 cm^3^ = 0.061 in^3^.

<br>

$$100\frac{g}{cm^3}*\frac{1kg}{1000g}*\frac{1cm^3}{0.061in^3}=1.639\frac{kg}{in^3}$$

:::

---

## {#unit-conv-practice data-menu-title="Unit conversions practice"} 

[Unit conversion practice]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
Practice dimensional analysis to perform the following conversions:

<br>

1. Convert $8.1\frac{km}{s}$ to miles per hour, given that 1 km = 0.621 miles.

<br>

2. Convert a mass flux of $3.2\frac{g}{min\cdot m^2}$ to $\frac{mg}{s\cdot cm^2}$.
:::

::: {.notes}
1. $8.1\frac{km}{s} \times \frac{60s}{min} \times \frac{60min}{hr} \times \frac{0.621 miles}{1km} = 18,108.36 \frac{miles}{hr}$

2. $3.2\frac{g}{min\cdot m^2} \times \frac{1min}{60s} \times \frac{1000 mg}{g} \times \frac{m}{100cm} \times \frac{m}{100cm}$
:::

---

## {#next-fxns data-menu-title="Next, functions"} 

[Math brain warm up]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
- Algebra blitz
- Exponentials and logarithms
- Common units and unit conversions
- [**Functions**]{.teal-text}
- Understanding graphs
- Interpreting equations
:::

---

## {#functions data-menu-title="Functions"} 

[Functions]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

[Functions are mathematical expressions that tell us how input values are related to output values.]{.body-text-m}

<br>

For example, $y = 3x-5$ is a function that tells us the **value of y** at **any value of x**. In this scenario, we would probably say **y is a function of x**. 

<br>

Could you also rewrite it and say **x is a function of y**? Here, with no knowledge of what's an input and what's an output, sure - but usually in environmental data science we specify the input variable(s), and the output variable(s) carefully. What follows is the expression in the format of: "**[output variable(s)] is/are a function of [input variable(s)]**". 

---

## {#inputs-outputs data-menu-title="Inputs & outputs"} 

[Thinking about inputs and outputs]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

For the following combinations of related variables, which do you expect would be the **input** and the **output** in a function describing how they are related? Say your answer in a sentence, e.g. "Evapotranspiration is a function of air temperature."

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
1. fuel (biomass) / slope / wildfire severity / windspeed / air temperature

2. wind speed / power generated by wind turbine

3. soil C:N ratio / bacterial biomass / soil water content / leaf litter decomposition rate
:::

::: {.notes}
1. wildfire severity is a function of fuel and slope and windspeed and air temperature
2. power generated by wind turbine is a function of wind speed
3. leaf litter decomposition rate is a function of soil C:N ratio and bacterial biomass and soil water content
:::

<!-- --- -->

<!-- ```{r} -->
<!-- #| eval: true -->
<!-- #| echo: false -->
<!-- #| out-width: "100%" -->
<!-- #| fig-align: "center" -->
<!-- knitr::include_graphics("images/betz_limit.png") -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- ::: {.center-text .body-text-s .gray-text} -->
<!-- Schaffarczyk A.P. (2020) Types of Wind Turbines. In: Introduction to Wind Turbine Aerodynamics. Green Energy & Technology. Springer, Cham. https://doi-org.proxy.library.ucsb.edu:9443/10.1007/978-3-030-41028-5_2 -->
<!-- ::: -->

---

## {#fxn-notation data-menu-title="Function notation"} 

[Function notation]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

[Single variable (univariate) function:]{.body-text-l} 

$f(x) = [expression\space containing \space x]$

<br>

[Multivariate function:]{.body-text-l} 

$g(a,T,z)=[expression\space containing \space a, T, \space and \space z]$

---

## {#eval-fxns data-menu-title="Evalutating functions"} 

[Evaluating functions]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
For continuous functions, we evaluate them by plugging in variable values. 

<br>

**Example:**

<br>

Evaluate $g(x,t)=2.4x+0.5t^2$ at $x = 3$ and $t = 10$

<br>

$g(3, 10) = 2.4(3) + 0.5(10^2)= 57.2$
:::

::: {.notes}
- $g(3,10) = 2.4 \times 3 + 0.5 \times 10^2$
- **$g(3,10) = 7.2 + 50 = 57.2$**
:::
